subject: Computer Networking
title: Mobile Data: How Phones Use the Internet
description: A quick, practical guide to Mobile Data: How Phones Use the Internet.

hook.text: You open an app on the bus. Data flows through cell towers, fiber cables, and servers in milliseconds.

buildup.text: Your phone connects to a nearby tower. The tower connects to the carrier's core network via fiber.

discovery.text: The core network routes your request to the internet. It also handles billing, security, and quality of service.

twist.text: Mobile data goes through more hops than Wi-Fi. That extra path adds latency — which is why Wi-Fi often feels faster.

climax.text: Carrier aggregation bonds multiple frequency bands together for faster speeds. Your phone uses several bands at once.

punchline.text: Mobile data is wireless at the edge, wired at the core.

quiz.question: What is carrier aggregation?
options: Combining multiple frequency bands for faster speeds
Switching between Wi-Fi and cellular automatically
Routing traffic through multiple cell towers simultaneously
